PLO Focus

Core Pathway: Fatty Acid and Triacylglycerol Synthesis

Everything below this divider belongs to the main synthesis objective until the LO 1 divider appears.

I

PLO: Fatty Acid & TAG Metabolism Overview

Fatty acid synthesis and triacylglycerol (TAG) synthesis are interconnected cytosolic processes that predominate in the fed state, primarily in the liver, and are orchestrated by insulin signaling.

▶ Slide 3 — FA & TAG Metabolism Roadmap
High-Yield Frame

FA synthesis and β-oxidation are reciprocally regulated. When one is active, the other is suppressed. The key switch is malonyl CoA: high malonyl CoA = FA synthesis ON, β-oxidation OFF (via CPT-I inhibition).

II

Getting Acetyl CoA to the Cytosol

FA synthesis is entirely cytosolic, but acetyl CoA is generated in the mitochondrial matrix. It cannot directly cross the inner mitochondrial membrane, so it uses the citrate shuttle.

1

Mitochondria: Acetyl CoA + OAA → Citrate

Citrate synthase condenses acetyl CoA with oxaloacetate (OAA) to form citrate. This is the same reaction as TCA cycle entry, but here the citrate exits the mitochondria instead of continuing in the cycle.

2

Citrate exits via tricarboxylate transporter

This antiporter in the inner mitochondrial membrane exports citrate to the cytosol in exchange for malate + phosphate.

3

Cytosol: Citrate → Acetyl CoA + OAA (ATP citrate lyase)

ATP citrate lyase (ACL) cleaves citrate back into acetyl CoA and OAA. This step requires ATP. ACL is insulin-induced (fed state enzyme).

4

OAA recycled → NADPH generated (malic enzyme)

OAA → malate (malate dehydrogenase) → pyruvate + CO₂ + NADPH (malic enzyme). The NADPH is used as reducing equivalents in FA synthesis. Pyruvate re-enters mitochondria to replenish OAA via pyruvate carboxylase.

NADPH Sources

FA synthesis requires 2 NADPH per elongation cycle. Cytosolic NADPH comes from: (1) the pentose phosphate pathway (glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase — insulin-induced) and (2) malic enzyme. Both are upregulated in the fed state.

III

Regulated Step: Acetyl CoA Carboxylase (ACC)

The committed, rate-limiting step of FA synthesis is the conversion of acetyl CoA to malonyl CoA by Acetyl CoA Carboxylase (ACC). This is where the cell decides whether to synthesize fat.

▶ Slide 4 — ACC Regulation: Allosteric + Covalent

The Reaction

ACC Reaction (requires biotin cofactor)

  • Substrate: Acetyl CoA + HCO₃⁻ + ATP
  • Product: Malonyl CoA + ADP + Pᵢ
  • Biotin is the CO₂ carrier (carboxyl group is added to the methyl end of acetyl CoA)
  • This is an irreversible, ATP-dependent carboxylation

ACC Regulation

Signal/Factor
Citrate (allosteric)
Long-chain FA acyl CoA (allosteric)
Insulin (covalent)
AMP (via AMPK)
Glucagon / Epinephrine
Insulin (transcriptional)
Activates ACC
Citrate: polymerizes inactive protomers → active polymer
Insulin → phosphatase → dephosphorylates ACC (active)
Induces ACC + FAS gene expression
Inhibits ACC
Palmitoyl CoA: product inhibition — reverses citrate polymerization
AMP → AMPK → phosphorylates ACC → inactive (energy-sensing)
Glucagon/Epi → ↑cAMP → PKA → activates AMPK cascade → phospho-ACC
▶ Slide 5 — Fed State: Insulin Upregulates ACC, FAS, G6PDH, Malic Enzyme, Citrate Lyase
▶ Slide 6 — Malonyl CoA Inhibits CPT-I (Prevents Simultaneous Synthesis + Degradation)
Critical Integration

Malonyl CoA as a metabolic switch: When ACC is active (fed state), malonyl CoA accumulates and allosterically inhibits CPT-I (carnitine palmitoyltransferase I). CPT-I is the rate-limiting step for importing long-chain FA into the mitochondria for β-oxidation. This ensures: you synthesize FA but don't simultaneously burn them.

IV

Fatty Acid Synthase (FAS) — The Elongation Cycle

FAS is a large multifunctional enzyme complex with 7 catalytic domains. It elongates a growing acyl chain by 2 carbons per cycle using malonyl CoA as the carbon donor.

▶ Slide 7 — FA Synthesis Mechanism

Starting the Chain

Primer & First Cycle

  • Primer: Acetyl CoA (2C) loads onto the cysteine-SH of the β-ketoacyl-ACP synthase (KS) domain
  • Carbon donor: Malonyl CoA (3C) loads onto the ACP domain via acyl carrier protein
  • In every elongation cycle, the 3C malonyl loses CO₂ — so each cycle adds net 2 carbons

The Four-Step Elongation Cycle

1

Condensation (β-ketoacyl-ACP synthase)

Acetyl group + malonyl-ACP → β-ketoacyl-ACP + CO₂. This decarboxylation releases the energy that drives the condensation forward.

2

Reduction (β-ketoacyl-ACP reductase) — NADPH

β-ketoacyl-ACP + NADPH → β-hydroxyacyl-ACP + NADP⁺

3

Dehydration (β-hydroxyacyl-ACP dehydratase)

β-hydroxyacyl-ACP → trans-enoyl-ACP + H₂O

4

Reduction (enoyl-ACP reductase) — NADPH

trans-enoyl-ACP + NADPH → acyl-ACP (fully saturated, now 2C longer) + NADP⁺

Product & Net Cost

After 7 cycles: Palmitate (C16:0) is released. Net equation starting from acetyl CoA:

8 Acetyl CoA + 7 ATP + 14 NADPH + 14 H⁺ → Palmitate + 8 CoA + 7 ADP + 7 Pᵢ + 14 NADP⁺ + 6 H₂O

Note: Synthesis vs Degradation are NOT simple reversal

The four steps of synthesis (condensation, reduction, dehydration, reduction) are mechanistically different from β-oxidation steps (dehydrogenation, hydration, dehydrogenation, thiolysis). They occur in different locations, use different carriers (ACP vs CoA), and use different redox coenzymes (NADPH vs NAD⁺/FAD).

V

Triacylglycerol (TAG) Synthesis

TAG synthesis esterifies three fatty acyl CoA molecules onto a glycerol-3-phosphate backbone. The tissue of origin determines how glycerol-3-phosphate is generated.

▶ Slide 8 — TAG Synthesis Pathway & Tissue Distribution

Glycerol-3-Phosphate Backbone — Tissue Difference

🫀 Liver
  • Has glycerol kinase
  • Glycerol → glycerol-3-P (via glycerol kinase)
  • Also uses DHAP from glycolysis
  • Packages TAG into VLDL for export
🫐 Adipose
  • Lacks glycerol kinase
  • Must use DHAP from glycolysis → glycerol-3-P
  • Needs glucose to esterify FAs (explains why insulin is required for fat storage)
  • Stores TAG as lipid droplets
💪 Muscle
  • Does not store TAG to any significant degree
  • Takes up FA from VLDL-TG hydrolysis by LPL
  • FA enters mitochondria → β-oxidation → CO₂ + H₂O + ATP

TAG Synthesis Steps

1

Glycerol-3-phosphate + FA₁CoA → lysophosphatidic acid

Glycerol-3-phosphate acyltransferase (GPAT) adds first fatty acid.

2

+ FA₂CoA → Phosphatidic acid

Second fatty acid added. Phosphatidic acid is a branch point — can go to phospholipids or TAGs.

3

Phosphatidic acid → Diacylglycerol (phosphatase removes Pᵢ)

Phosphatidate phosphatase (lipin) removes the phosphate group.

4

Diacylglycerol + FA₃CoA → Triacylglycerol

Diacylglycerol acyltransferase (DGAT) adds the third fatty acid. DGAT is the committed step for TAG synthesis specifically.

VLDL Export & LPL

In the liver, newly synthesized TAG is packaged with apoB-100 and phospholipids into VLDL, secreted into blood. At capillaries of adipose and muscle, lipoprotein lipase (LPL) hydrolyzes VLDL-TG → free FA + glycerol. FA taken up by: muscle (oxidation) or adipose (re-esterification into TAG for storage). Glycerol returns to liver (gluconeogenesis substrate).

VI

Synthesis vs. Degradation — The Master Comparison

These two pathways are reciprocally regulated, compartmentalized, and use distinct enzymes, carriers, and coenzymes — making them a high-yield exam target.

▶ Slide 9 — Fatty Acid Synthesis vs Degradation Comparison Table
Variable 🟢 Synthesis 🔴 Degradation (β-oxidation)
Greatest fluxAfter carbohydrate-rich mealIn starvation
Hormonal stateHigh insulin/glucagon ratioLow insulin/glucagon ratio
Major tissue sitePrimarily liverMuscle, liver
Subcellular locationCytosolPrimarily mitochondria
Acyl group carrier (mitochondria ↔ cytosol)Citrate (mitochondria → cytosol)Carnitine (cytosol → mitochondria)
Phosphopantetheine carrierACP domain of FAS + CoACoenzyme A only
Redox coenzymeNADPH (reduction)NAD⁺, FAD (oxidation)
2-carbon unitMalonyl CoA (donor)Acetyl CoA (product)
ActivatorCitrate (activates ACC)—
InhibitorPalmitoyl CoA (inhibits ACC)Malonyl CoA (inhibits CPT-I)
End productPalmitateAcetyl CoA
Repetitive 4-step processCondensation → Reduction → Dehydration → ReductionDehydrogenation → Hydration → Dehydrogenation → Thiolysis
LO 1 Focus

Insulin Resistance in Liver, Skeletal Muscle, and Adipose

This next block is the insulin-resistance objective, organized by tissue so the consequences stay easy to separate.

LO 1

Describe Tissue-Specific Insulin Resistance

This objective is organized by liver, skeletal muscle, and adipose so you can tie each defect to the metabolic consequence it creates.

  • Liver: continued gluconeogenesis with preserved lipogenesis
  • Skeletal muscle: impaired GLUT4-mediated glucose disposal and lipotoxic signaling
  • Adipose: failed suppression of lipolysis with FFA spillover and inflammation
VII

LO 1: Insulin Resistance & Tissue-Specific Effects

Insulin resistance is defined as a reduced biological response to normal insulin concentrations. Crucially, resistance is not uniform — different signaling arms of the insulin receptor become resistant at different rates, creating paradoxical metabolic consequences.

Upstream Signaling Context

Insulin → binds receptor tyrosine kinase → autophosphorylation → IRS-1/2 phosphorylation (tyrosine) → PI3K → PIP3 → Akt (PKB) activation. Akt is the master effector: it drives GLUT4 translocation, glycogen synthesis, lipogenesis (via SREBP-1c), and suppresses gluconeogenesis (via phosphorylation/exclusion of FOXO1). In insulin resistance, this cascade is impaired at the IRS-1 level (often via serine phosphorylation of IRS-1, which blocks tyrosine phosphorylation).

Liver — Selective Insulin Resistance

What Becomes Resistant

  • Insulin normally suppresses gluconeogenesis by phosphorylating and excluding FOXO1 from the nucleus (FOXO1 drives PEPCK and G6Pase expression)
  • In insulin resistance: FOXO1 remains active → gluconeogenesis continues uninhibited → fasting hyperglycemia
  • Glycogen synthesis impaired (reduced glycogen synthase activation)

What Remains Sensitive (The Paradox)

  • Insulin-driven activation of SREBP-1c (sterol regulatory element binding protein 1c) remains intact
  • SREBP-1c induces ACC, FAS, and other lipogenic enzymes → de novo lipogenesis continues
  • Result: hyperglycemia (from unrestrained gluconeogenesis) + hypertriglyceridemia (from excess VLDL secretion)
  • This is the metabolic basis of non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
Clinical Pearl — Selective Hepatic Insulin Resistance

The liver is simultaneously insulin-resistant for glucose metabolism but insulin-sensitive for lipid synthesis. This explains why insulin-resistant patients have high fasting glucose AND high triglycerides — a hallmark of metabolic syndrome.

Skeletal Muscle — Impaired Glucose Disposal

Key Defects

  • Reduced GLUT4 translocation to plasma membrane → ↓ glucose uptake (skeletal muscle accounts for ~80% of postprandial glucose disposal)
  • Impaired glycogen synthesis (glycogen synthase kinase-3/GSK-3 not properly inhibited)
  • Intramyocellular lipid (IMCL) accumulation: excess circulating FFAs enter muscle, form diacylglycerol (DAG) and ceramide
  • DAG activates PKC-θ → serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 → blocks insulin receptor signaling (feed-forward inhibition)
  • Ceramide activates phosphatase PP2A → dephosphorylates Akt → blocks downstream signaling
Lipotoxicity Mechanism

Elevated plasma FFAs → ectopic fat deposition in muscle and liver → DAG/ceramide accumulation → PKC activation → serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 → insulin resistance. This is why visceral obesity (high circulating FFA from adipose lipolysis) strongly predicts insulin resistance.

Adipose Tissue — Dysregulated Lipolysis

Key Defects

  • Reduced GLUT4 translocation → ↓ glucose uptake, ↓ glycerol-3-P for FA re-esterification
  • Insulin normally suppresses lipolysis by inhibiting hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) (via phosphodiesterase activation → ↓ cAMP → ↓ PKA → ↓ HSL phosphorylation)
  • In insulin resistance: HSL is not properly suppressed → excessive lipolysis → massive FFA release into circulation
  • Elevated plasma FFAs → ectopic fat deposition (liver, muscle) → worsens insulin resistance systemically
  • Adipose also becomes pro-inflammatory: ↑ TNF-α, IL-6 from hypertrophied adipocytes → further serine phosphorylation of IRS-1 throughout body
  • ↓ adiponectin production → loss of insulin sensitization
The Vicious Cycle

Adipose insulin resistance → ↑ lipolysis → ↑ FFA → muscle and liver lipotoxicity → worsened insulin resistance everywhere → compensatory hyperinsulinemia → further adipose dysfunction. The fat tissue drives the metabolic syndrome.

LO 2 Focus

Fructose as a Driver of Metabolic Disease

This final content block is the fructose objective and explains why hepatic fructose metabolism promotes lipogenesis and poor satiety signaling.

LO 2

Discuss Fructose in Metabolic Disease

The high-yield frame is that fructose is handled mainly by the liver, bypasses key glycolytic regulation, and strongly promotes lipogenesis and poor satiety signaling.

  • Normal fructose absorption and hepatic metabolism
  • Why fructose bypasses PFK-1-centered control
  • How fructose drives TAG synthesis, NAFLD, and insulin resistance
  • Why fructose does not create the same satiety response as glucose
VIII

LO 2: Fructose Metabolism & Metabolic Disease

Dietary fructose — primarily from added sugars (HFCS, sucrose) — is metabolized almost entirely by the liver through a pathway that lacks the regulatory checkpoints of glucose metabolism, making it a powerful driver of metabolic disease.

Normal Fructose Metabolism

1

Absorption: GLUT5 (small intestine)

Fructose is absorbed via GLUT5 (not GLUT4). It does NOT require insulin for uptake. It is transported to the liver via the portal circulation (some gut metabolism occurs but most reaches liver).

2

Liver: Fructose → Fructose-1-phosphate (fructokinase)

Fructokinase (ketohexokinase) phosphorylates fructose at C-1. Critical: fructokinase has NO feedback inhibition by its product and is not regulated by energy status. This allows uncontrolled phosphorylation that can deplete intracellular ATP and phosphate rapidly.

3

Fructose-1-phosphate → DHAP + Glyceraldehyde (aldolase B)

Aldolase B (liver-specific) cleaves fructose-1-phosphate into DHAP and glyceraldehyde. Glyceraldehyde is phosphorylated by triose kinase → glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

4

DHAP + G3P enter glycolysis/gluconeogenesis/lipogenesis

These triose phosphates bypass the major regulatory steps of glycolysis: glucokinase (not induced for fructose) and PFK-1 (bypassed entirely). They flood downstream metabolic pathways.

Why Fructose Bypasses Regulation

Glucose is regulated at two major checkpoints: glucokinase (by glucose concentration) and PFK-1 (by ATP, citrate, AMP). Fructose enters as fructose-1-phosphate, which bypasses PFK-1 entirely. This means the cell has no effective brake on fructose metabolism — all of it must be processed, overwhelming the liver's capacity.

How Fructose Drives Metabolic Disease

1. De Novo Lipogenesis (DNL)

  • Triose phosphates → acetyl CoA → malonyl CoA → palmitate → TAG
  • High fructose dramatically increases hepatic DNL
  • Excess TAG secreted as VLDL → hypertriglyceridemia
  • Intrahepatic fat accumulation → NAFLD

2. Uric Acid Generation (ATP Depletion)

  • Fructokinase phosphorylates fructose using ATP rapidly → AMP accumulates
  • AMP → IMP → hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid (via xanthine oxidase)
  • Uric acid → gout, hypertension (inhibits eNOS → ↓ NO → endothelial dysfunction), renal disease
  • Uric acid also directly promotes insulin resistance in hepatocytes

3. Glycerol-3-Phosphate → Gluconeogenesis & More TAG

  • DHAP → glycerol-3-phosphate → backbone for TAG synthesis (fat storage) OR gluconeogenesis → hyperglycemia
  • Combined with impaired insulin signaling: worsens fasting glucose

4. Hepatic Insulin Resistance

  • Increased DNL generates DAG → activates PKC → serine phosphorylation of IRS-1/2 → insulin resistance
  • High fructose diet → NAFLD → hepatic insulin resistance → unrestrained gluconeogenesis (FOXO1 active)

5. No Satiety Signaling

  • Fructose does NOT stimulate insulin secretion (no glucose-stimulated insulin release pathway)
  • Fructose does NOT suppress ghrelin (hunger hormone) effectively
  • Fructose does NOT stimulate leptin release
  • Result: fructose-rich foods do not generate satiety → overconsumption → caloric excess
Clinical Takeaway — Metabolic Syndrome

Chronic high fructose intake (HFCS in sodas, processed foods) drives: NAFLD → hepatic insulin resistance → hyperglycemia → dyslipidemia (↑TG, ↓HDL) → hypertension → gout. This is the metabolic syndrome constellation, and dietary fructose is the unifying upstream driver in modern diets.

Hereditary Fructose Intolerance (HFI) — Bonus Boards

Aldolase B deficiency. Fructose-1-phosphate accumulates → depletes hepatic ATP and phosphate → hypoglycemia (inhibits both gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis), vomiting, liver failure. Patients develop aversion to sweet foods. Avoid fructose, sorbitol, sucrose.

Practice Questions — Dr. Spicer Style

Click an answer choice to check. Styled after Dr. Spicer's question architecture: mechanism-first, integration-focused, clinically anchored.
1. A patient is in the fed state with elevated insulin levels. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which insulin activates Acetyl CoA Carboxylase (ACC)?
A. Insulin directly phosphorylates ACC at a serine residue, converting it to the active polymer form
B. Insulin increases cytosolic citrate concentrations, which allosterically polymerizes ACC
C. Insulin activates a phosphatase that dephosphorylates ACC, converting it to its active form
D. Insulin induces transcription of the ACC gene, acutely increasing enzyme abundance
E. Insulin promotes accumulation of palmitoyl CoA, which allosterically activates ACC
2. Acetyl CoA generated in the mitochondrial matrix must be transported to the cytosol for fatty acid synthesis. Which of the following correctly describes this transport mechanism?
A. Acetyl CoA binds carnitine to form acetylcarnitine, which is transported across the inner mitochondrial membrane
B. Acetyl CoA condenses with OAA to form citrate, which exits via the tricarboxylate transporter; ATP citrate lyase then regenerates acetyl CoA in the cytosol
C. Acetyl CoA is directly transported across the inner mitochondrial membrane via a specific acetyl-CoA translocase
D. Acetyl CoA is converted to malate, which crosses the membrane; malate dehydrogenase then regenerates acetyl CoA in the cytosol
E. Pyruvate exits the mitochondria and is decarboxylated in the cytosol by a cytosolic pyruvate dehydrogenase complex
3. A liver cell has high concentrations of malonyl CoA. Which of the following is the most direct consequence of this elevated malonyl CoA?
A. Increased flux through fatty acid synthase, producing more palmitate
B. Inhibition of ATP citrate lyase, reducing cytosolic acetyl CoA availability
C. Allosteric inhibition of CPT-I, preventing long-chain fatty acids from entering the mitochondria for β-oxidation
D. Direct inhibition of AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK), reducing phosphorylation of metabolic enzymes
E. Inhibition of CPT-II, preventing reconstitution of acyl-CoA from acylcarnitine in the mitochondrial matrix
4. A patient with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with fasting hyperglycemia and a serum triglyceride level of 450 mg/dL. His liver biopsy shows significant steatosis. Which of the following best explains the coexistence of both hyperglycemia AND hypertriglyceridemia in hepatic insulin resistance?
A. Insulin resistance uniformly impairs all insulin signaling in the liver, reducing both glucose production and VLDL secretion equally
B. Excess glucagon directly stimulates both gluconeogenesis and de novo lipogenesis simultaneously
C. Selective insulin resistance affects only the FOXO1 (anti-gluconeogenic) arm, while SREBP-1c lipogenesis remains insulin-sensitive, causing hyperglycemia and hypertriglyceridemia simultaneously
D. The high glucose levels directly activate ChREBP, which simultaneously drives gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis
E. Hepatic lipase deficiency impairs VLDL clearance, causing TG accumulation, while simultaneously impaired glycogen synthesis causes hyperglycemia
5. Compared to glucose, dietary fructose is a more potent driver of de novo lipogenesis and metabolic syndrome. Which of the following best explains this difference?
A. Fructose is absorbed more slowly than glucose and requires insulin-mediated GLUT4 for uptake, creating a sustained metabolic burden
B. Fructose is metabolized primarily in skeletal muscle via glycolysis, generating excess acetyl CoA that is exported to the liver
C. Fructose is converted to fructose-1-phosphate by unregulated fructokinase and cleaved to triose phosphates by aldolase B, bypassing PFK-1 regulation and flooding the liver with lipogenic precursors
D. Fructose potently stimulates insulin secretion, which then drives SREBP-1c activation and lipogenesis in the liver
E. Fructose is primarily converted to fructose-6-phosphate in the liver, which enters glycolysis at a step that bypasses glucokinase regulation
6. During a carbohydrate-rich meal, fatty acid synthesis occurs in the cytosol of hepatocytes while β-oxidation is simultaneously inhibited. Which pair of molecules best represents the allosteric activator of fatty acid synthesis and the resulting inhibitor of β-oxidation, respectively?
A. Palmitoyl CoA activates ACC; AMP inhibits CPT-I
B. Citrate activates ACC; malonyl CoA inhibits CPT-I
C. Insulin activates ACC; insulin inhibits CPT-I directly
D. NADPH activates ACC; palmitoyl CoA inhibits CPT-I
E. Malonyl CoA activates ACC; AMP inhibits CPT-I
7. Adipose tissue becomes insulin resistant in obesity. Which of the following best explains how adipose insulin resistance worsens insulin resistance in other tissues such as the liver and skeletal muscle?
B. Adipose atrophy reduces leptin secretion, which normally suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis
A. Adipose insulin resistance causes cortisol hypersecretion, which directly induces hepatic gluconeogenesis
C. Impaired insulin suppression of HSL leads to excess FFA release into circulation; FFA deposition in liver and muscle causes DAG accumulation, PKC activation, and serine phosphorylation of IRS-1
D. Glycerol released by adipose lipolysis directly inhibits the insulin receptor tyrosine kinase in the liver
E. Increased adiponectin secretion from hypertrophied adipocytes inhibits GLUT4 translocation in skeletal muscle
